title: Pocket Solar USB Phone Charger
description: A palm-sized solar charger that tops up your phone anywhere the sun shines. Built from a 5V solar panel and a USB buck converter module — a perfect first electronics build.
creator: Ben Builds

steps.text: Test the solar panel with a multimeter in direct sunlight — you should see 5–6V open circuit.
Solder the panel's positive lead to the buck converter's VIN+ pad and negative to VIN−.
Verify 5.0–5.2V at the converter's USB output under sunlight before connecting any device.
Mount the converter behind the panel with hot glue, leaving the USB port accessible.
Enclose the electronics, leaving the panel face exposed. Add a keychain loop if you like.
Test-charge an old phone first, and monitor for heat during the first full charge.

safetyWarnings: Always verify output voltage with a multimeter before plugging in a phone.
Do not leave charging devices unattended in direct hot sun.

title: PVC-Blade Backyard Wind Turbine
description: A small wind turbine using blades cut from PVC pipe and a DC treadmill motor as the generator. Charges a 12V battery in steady wind — a classic off-grid starter project.
creator: Red Barn Artisan | Mark Patrick

steps.text: Cut three identical curved blades from the PVC pipe using a printable template — the pipe's curve gives the airfoil shape.
Drill and bolt the blades to the hub at equal 120° spacing. Balance the rotor on a nail — sand heavy blades until it sits level.
Mount the hub on the treadmill motor shaft. Spin it by hand and confirm voltage output on the multimeter.
Bolt the motor to a yaw platform with the plywood tail vane so it turns into the wind.
Mount the assembly on the mast with guy wires. Keep it above roof turbulence if possible.
Wire through the charge controller to the 12V battery. Never run the turbine unloaded in high wind — it can overspeed.

safetyWarnings: Spinning blades can cause serious injury — mount well above head height.
Always connect a load or brake before high winds; unloaded turbines overspeed and can shed blades.
Battery charging produces hydrogen gas — ventilate the battery area.

title: Candle-Powered Thermoelectric Phone Charger
description: Uses a Peltier/TEG module sandwiched between a candle-heated plate and a heatsink to generate real electricity from a temperature difference. Slow but genuinely works — great physics demo.
creator: Spang420

steps.text: Apply thermal paste and sandwich the TEG modules between the aluminum plate (hot side) and the heatsink (cold side).
Clamp the sandwich with bolts at the corners — good contact pressure matters more than anything else.
Wire the two TEGs in series and connect to the boost converter input.
Build a small stand so a tea light sits about 2 cm below the aluminum plate.
Light the candle, wait 2–3 minutes for the temperature difference to develop, and check the 5V USB output.
For more power, add a small fan on the heatsink (powered by the TEG itself) to keep the cold side cold.

safetyWarnings: Open flame — never leave unattended, keep away from flammable materials.
The hot plate exceeds 200°C — burn hazard.
TEC modules (vs true TEGs) can be damaged above ~150°C hot-side.

title: Hand-Crank Emergency Generator
description: A geared DC motor with a crank handle that charges small devices or a battery bank by hand. Great emergency-kit project and a solid intro to generators.
creator: GreatScott!

steps.text: Bolt the geared motor to the plywood base so it won't twist while cranking.
Attach the crank handle to the motor's output shaft.
Wire motor output through the bridge rectifier so polarity is correct regardless of crank direction.
Add the smoothing capacitor, then the 5V USB regulator.
Crank at a steady comfortable pace and confirm 5V at the USB port with a multimeter.
Optional: charge a small 12V SLA or power bank instead so devices see clean, steady power.

safetyWarnings: Charge a power bank rather than a phone directly — hand cranking gives uneven voltage.

title: Bicycle Pedal Power Station
description: Converts an old bike + training stand into a pedal generator that can run lights, charge batteries, or power a laptop. Real sustained human power output: 50–150W.
creator: Backyard Trail Builds

steps.text: Mount the bike on the training stand and position the scooter motor so its roller presses against the rear tire.
Fabricate a spring-tensioned bracket so the roller keeps firm contact with the tire.
Pedal and measure open-circuit voltage — gearing should give 14–20V at a comfortable cadence.
Wire motor → fuse → charge controller → battery. The battery buffers your uneven pedaling.
Connect the inverter to the battery terminals with thick gauge wire.
Run your load from the inverter. Track watt output with an inline meter to gamify workouts.

safetyWarnings: Inverter output is 120V AC — same shock hazard as a wall outlet.
Always fuse the battery line; lead-acid batteries can deliver hundreds of amps into a short.
Batteries vent hydrogen when charging — keep sparks away and ventilate.

title: Soda Can Solar Air Heater
description: A window-mounted panel of black-painted aluminum cans that heats room air using pure sunlight — no electricity generated, but real free heating that can cut winter bills.
creator: desertsun02

steps.text: Drill or punch holes through the bottom of each can so air can flow through them.
Glue cans into vertical columns (tubes) with high-temp silicone — typically 6 columns of 12 cans.
Build the plywood box with an air inlet at the bottom and outlet at the top.
Paint the can columns flat black and mount them inside the box.
Seal the clear glazing over the front. Air gaps kill efficiency — caulk everything.
Mount facing south (northern hemisphere) and duct the outlet through a window panel. Add the fan for better flow.

safetyWarnings: Outlet air can exceed 60°C on bright days — use ducting rated for heat.
Sharp can edges — wear gloves during prep.

title: "Magnet + Copper Coil Free Energy Light Bulb"
description: A viral build claiming a magnet taped inside a copper coil lights a bulb forever with no power source. We catalog it because it's everywhere on TikTok — read the claim check before wasting your money.
creator: ElectroBOOM (debunk video)

steps.text: This build is documented for educational purposes — it does not produce energy.
A stationary magnet inside a coil induces zero current. Faraday's law requires a CHANGING magnetic field (the magnet must move relative to the coil).
Viral videos of this 'working' use hidden button-cell batteries inside the bulb base or cuts in the footage.
Want the real version? Spin the magnet with wind, water, or a hand crank — that's just... a generator! See our legit builds.

safetyWarnings: The main hazard here is to your wallet.
